pattern: The count of perfect k-th powers at most some value grows monotonically as that value grows, so the smallest and largest qualifying base can each be pinned down with a binary search instead of a scan.
hints: Rather than testing every integer in the range for being a perfect power, think in terms of the base: which integers x, when raised to the k-th power, land inside [l, r]?
Binary search for the smallest base whose k-th power is at least l, and separately for the largest base whose k-th power is at most r; the count of qualifying bases is just the gap between them.

When raising a candidate base to the k-th power, multiply one factor at a time and stop as soon as the running product exceeds the bound you are comparing against, so you never need to compute an astronomically large power in full.

edge_inputs.description: a range containing no perfect power of the given kind
a range where the base itself must be zero
the exponent at its minimum, every integer counts as a first power
the exponent at its maximum allowed value
the full allowed value range with a small exponent
l and r equal to the same perfect power exactly
the range boundary sits one below a perfect power, excluding it

stress.description: random ranges and exponents across the full allowed scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    bound = max(1, min(size, 1000000000))
    k = rng.randint(1, 30)
    a = rng.randint(0, bound)
    b = rng.randint(0, bound)
    l, r = (a, b) if a <= b else (b, a)
    return [l, r, k]
